content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies with people stuck in one location

**Score:** 104
**Body:** Need some suggestions about movies where they are in one location most of the movie, kind of similar to The Ruins, Dawn of the dead, Legion, Belko Experiment, Splinter.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Phone Booth
2. 10 Cloverfield Lane (2016)
3. The Thing

The hateful 8 for the most part
4. Rear Window and Rope. Hitchcock classics!
5. Coherence
6. 12 Angry Men
7. Moon
8. Clue
9. Cube
10. The Lighthouse 

## 2. Please suggest some great films about men in a downward spiral.

**Score:** 100
**Body:** I’m putting together a list of films about men in a downward spiral, and I’d love to hear your recommendations. Some of the films I’ve really enjoyed so far include:

Scarface – 1983
Goodfellas – 1990
Bad Lieutenant – 1992
Carlito’s Way – 1993
Casino – 1995
Heat – 1995
Rounders – 1998
American Psycho – 2000
The Gambler – 2014
Uncut Gems – 2019

Thank you for your suggestions. 🫶
Edited: Edit: I’ve been compiling a list of great films about men in a downward spiral, and I want to sincerely thank everyone who has contributed suggestions. Your input is genuinely appreciated and has made this list much richer and more thoughtful. I now have the perfect list—thank you for taking the time to share your recommendations.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. falling down. 

he's a bit spiraled before it starts but it gets worse.
  - That was my choice as well and the poster child for this topic.

Leaving Las Vegas (Nicholas Cage) is another one I might put in the list
  - Thank you. Michael Douglas is so great in this film. On the list.
2. Leaving Las Vegas
  - Maybe the most accurate depiction of severe alcoholism ever made.
3. Boogie Nights (1997) is definitely one.
4. Control, the movie about Ian Curtis, the lead singer of Joy Division
5. A Different Man (2024)

The Machinist

Drive

Dallas Buyers Club
6. All That Jazz. Requiem for a Dream. Lenny (1974). Taxi Driver. A Star is Born.
7. The Wrestler

## 3. Movies like Little Women (2019) & Good Will Hunting & Dead Poets Society & The Wild Robot & The Holdovers

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Hi all! It's me again with the same question.

I'm back to ask you for similar movie recommendations as the ones above. I want something that makes me appreciate film, poetry, writing, life and all it encapsulates. I want to feel so deeply and be left speechless by the overall beauty of the film. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Perks of Being a Wallflower, Before Sunrise, Manchester by the Sea.
2. Mona Lisa Smile
3. Yesterday
4. Only Yesterday (1991)  
The Straight Story (1999)  
The Station Agent (2003)
5.  “Possession” make sure it is the 2002 film titled “Possession.” It’s not scary or anything. Not even creepy. It’s a love story about poetry, poets and academics and another love story. I really enjoyed it but it’s directed by Neil LaBute but isn’t considered one of his better films. 

“Three Thousand Years of Longing.” Directed by George Miller of Mad Max fame. But, he also directed Babe and Happy Feet. ButThree Thousand…” is about an academic and a djinn and the nature of myth and storytelling and love. 

“Wonder Boys” Directed by Curtis Hanson; from À novel by Michael Chabon. About a professor and writer and a crazy weekend as one man’s life goes through changes. One of my favorite books and movies. The movie is faithful to the novel. Michael Douglas and Robert Downey Jr. are both great. 

The first two are based on works by A.S. Byatt. 

“Where the Buffalo Roam” and “Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas” are both about Hunter S. Thompson and ostensibly writing. But “Buffalo” is more about Thompson and his writing while “Fear and Loathing” is based on the book. 

“Deathtrap” is a dark comedy about a play write and his student and involves the writing and stealing of a play. Made in 1982.
6. Tuesdays with Morrie
7. Remember The Titans
8. The Way Way Back… Steve Carrell and Toni Collete

Went into it expecting totally different movie and was moved to tears
9. Blinded By The Light (2019). It's based on the real life experiences of a working class Pakistani teenage in 1980sUK who discovers with Bruce Springsteen's songs. It's exactly what your looking for. The main character is a poet and about how the songwriting of a man, who he thought was very different from him, resonates with him and changes his perspective on life.
10. Much Ado About Nothing (1993)

The Secret Garden (1993)

## 4. Movies that are spooky but not scary?

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I want to start getting in the Halloween spirit, but I'm also a coward. Are there any movies that have a spooky vibe but not very scary? I really liked the night before Christmas and LOVED hocus pocus.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Paranorman, Coraline, Corpse Bride, Halloween Tree, Don’t Look Under the Bed, Under Wraps, Mom’s Got a Date with a Vampire, Frankenstein and Me, The Monster Squad, Dracula Dead and Loving It, Young Frankenstein, Casper, Gremlins, Monster House, and Frankenweenie

I don’t think you could go wrong with any of those
2. Trick r Treat 2007
  - my favorite Halloween movie for sure! We always watch it on Halloween night because it has perfect vibes for it
3. Haunted Mansion is another fun one that I don’t think has been listed yet. I especially love the muppet version.
4. The Ghost and Mister Chicken (1966)
5. Casper

Hubie Halloween

Halloweentown

The Witches of Eastwick

The Witches (1990)

Coraline 

Pretty much any Tim Burton movie

Labyrinth
6. I think Over the Garden Wall is easily the perfect Halloween show. It’s a series of 10 episodes, each about 11 minutes long. It’s only like 1hr 50min all together and hands down has the most Halloween/Fall vibes of any show/movie I’ve seen. Please give it a watch!

If you liked The Nightmare Before Christmas, check out Coraline (same director) or Corpse Bride and Frankenweenie which is Tim Burton.

Random fun Halloween movies based off the ones you mentioned:

- Trick r Treat (my fav Halloween movie ever)
- Hocus Pocus 2
- The Addams Family
- Beetlejuice
- Ghostbusters
- Hubie Halloween
7. I think[The Lady In White](https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lady_in_White) fits the bill.
1998 movie about a kid that accidentally gets locked in a school closet overnight and is visited by the eerie ghost of a murdered girl.


Not sure if Stir of Echoes would fit, but it's a good one. It's definitely spooky and suspenseful. No jump scares or gore or demons but ghots for sure. Lol



The Lovely Bones was spooky and sad but good and suspenseful and eerie.
  - Yes, Lady in White! Also The Others and Something Wicked this way comes
8. The Burbs

## 5. Movies where the bad guy pretends to be police

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Need help finding as many examples as I can! Movies or popular tv shows! Older is good, doesn't have to be new

Bad guy impersonates / dresses up as a cop:

* Home Alone (Joe Pesci) 
* The Dark Knight (Joker)
* The Jackal (Bruce Willis)
* Skyfall (Silva) 

  
What am I missing? Thanks in advance!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Terminator 2 (1991)  
The Silence of the Lambs (1991) (ambulance scene)  
Surveillance (2008)
  - Terminator 2 is a must include, easily the most iconic instance of this happening.

Depending on what you're working on, OP, you might also want to use this quote from James Cameron:

> "The Terminator films are not really about the human race getting killed of by future machines. They're about us losing touch with our own humanity and becoming machines, which allows us to kill and brutalize each other," he says. "Cops think all non-cops as less than they are, stupid, weak, and evil. They dehumanize the people they are sworn to protect and desensitize themselves in order to do that job."
2. The Departed

Infernal Affairs
3. No Country for Old Men
4. 1. The town (Ben affleck and Jeremy renner)
 

2. Die hard with a vengeance - short scene in elevator

3. Rush hour (kidnap scene)

4. Collateral damage (bombing scene)

5. Con air
5. Blue Streak(1999)
6. Part of me wants to say Training Day because Harris was more criminal than a cop. Also, The Departed.
7. Banshee tv series
8. Miami Blues 1990 Fred Ward Alec Baldwin.
9. Identity

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What is something unrealistic that you often see in movies that annoys the hell out of you?

**Score:** 6310
**Body:** 
Oh boy. Where do I start? Let’s start with the list.

I hate how every lock can be broken by shooting a single round at it.

When they dive through glass panes and come out without a scratch on the other side.

People turning their heads around to talk for extended periods while driving.

When government agents show up in a foreign country and have a shoot out with the bad guys out in public. The shoot out ends, the world moves on, and the government agents return back to the office to talk about what to do next.

Female action stars performing crazy action scenes without their hair tied up out of their face.
In horror movies when someone's running from the bad guy and they fall and start crawling. GET UP AND RUN, STUPID.

The incredible ability to zoom in to photographs and/or CCTV footage to an almost cellular level and STILL have a crystal clear image.

That strangling people kills them in a few seconds. A guy can strangle someone to death in 15 seconds and move on. In reality, it can take full minutes of strangling someone to kill them. They never get tired from all that effort.

Every relationship in pretty much every chickflick
Flawless teeth during medieval times.
Those big ass breakfasts the mom will make and then the kids will take two bites and run out the house.

And the list goes on and on! Comment your favourite:)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Gold, they seem to be able to transport or carry gold like it’s nothing.
2. All vehicles in an abandoned location have keys, gas, and batteries charged.
  - Just check the sun visor.
3. When all the problems in the film could've been averted by a one sentence explanation, but they never say it.

"The woman that you saw me hugging was my sister"

That sort of thing.
  - In a similar vein. When a someone says "wait, i have to tell you something important" and the other one replies "i dont have time for that now"
4. People crawling through air ducts.  You can’t pick one up without making a tremendous bloody racket
  - That and air ducts actually holding your weight
5. Gun shots indoors not making everyone deaf
6. I need blood for a ritual, or to throw off my enemy. I better slice *THE PALM OF MY HAND* to get it. That won't cripple me during a time when I'm fighting for my life or anything.
  - I've always hated those scenes where they need blood and make like a 3 inch long slice in their palm rather than just a small cut on any other part of their body

## 2. What movie “detail” took you several rewatches to notice?

**Score:** 3455

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I have to tell you something about Captain Hook and Mr. Darling...
  - In the Disney cartoon?
    - Most versions actually, but the Disney cartoon did that as well…
2. I saw The Wizard of Oz several times as a kid, but I didn’t notice until I saw it as an adult that the farm hands were Dorothy’s companions in Oz. 😂
  - I think this happened to a lot of kids! We were so focused on the actual Oz part that we didn’t pay closer attention to the supporting cast in Kansas.
3. In the 6th Sense, the dude in the hairpiece was Bruce Willis the whole time.
4. Alan Grant's makeshift buckle using two *female* ends on the helicopter ride to Jurassic Park foreshadows the later revelation that despite all being bred female, the dinosaurs have managed to find a way to breed uncontrollably amongst themselves ("Life finds a way...").
  - For the longest time I thought it was just a joke about how he sucks with technology of any kind
5. In knives Out the portrait of the old man changes from a scowl to a smirk once the mystery is revealed.
6. I had seen Batman Returns probably 100 times including several times in the theater but it wasn't until I was in my mid to late 30s that I noticed that the Mayor's costume at Max's costume ball is a knife in his back because of the way Max stabbed him in the back with the failed Penguin election scheme.  

[https://i.imgur.com/u6Fnjrh.jpg](https://i.imgur.com/u6Fnjrh.jpg)

## 3. What movie line was delivered so flawlessly it deserved it's own Oscar award?

**Score:** 1117
**Body:** Jack Nicholson for A few good men (1992)

"You can't handle the truth!"...

*sends chills down your spine everytime!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I prefer Nicholson's delivery of "YOU'RE GOD DAMN RIGHT I DID!". You can really feel the venom in his voice.
2. "YES they deserve to die and I hope they burn in hell!!"
  - "Can you see her? I want you to picture that little girl. Now imagine she's white."
3. "Am I not merciful?"

When I heard it for the first time, I got shivers down my spine
4. LOTR:

"Then let's be done with it, once and for all. Come on Mr. Frodo! I CAN'T CARRY IT FOR YOU, BUT I CAN CARRY YOU!!"  🌋☄

Edit: punctuation
5. "When the fuck did we get ice cream?"
6. Well ... no shit. What have we got here, a fucking comedian? Private Joker? I admire your honesty. Hell, I like you. You can come over to my house and fuck my sister.
7. All those moments will be lost in time, like tears in rain. Time to die.
8. EVERYONE!!!
9. “You are sheltering enemies of the state are you not” 

Actually won an Oscar but should get another for that bit

## 4. Best supporting character in a film.  Who’s yours?

**Score:** 437
**Body:** Serge in Beverly Hills Cop, was just a huge delight.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Get the fuck outta here! x
  - No it’s true I sell it myself!
  - No I can noooot!
  - Axel meeting Serg for the first time was such a perfect scene. And I think I read somewhere that it was mostly improvised, which makes it even more impressive.    
I love how they’re from such different cultures that they may as well be space aliens to each other, but  they share the trait of being “pleasant fellows” and get along just fine.  
Also there’s something very dignified about how they’re both portrayed, despite being caricatures.
2. thats Balki
3. We read his name in his voice. "Ack-whell?"
4. Val Kilmer as Elvis in True Romance
5. I loved Beverly Hills cop three. And I thought that the gun that Serge sells was so awesome.
6. Walter Sobchak
7. You vant it wis a lemon twist?

## 5. Casino Royale was shot on 35mm film, Skyfall on digital. Do you think it makes a difference? Which movie in your opinion looks better?

**Score:** 368

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The one with Eva Green onscreen
  - I've always been a sucker for good eye makeup.
    - Take me back to the bold eyeliner of the early 00's please
      - Avril Lavigne just popped into my head
2. I think Skyfall had more shots that intended to be awing. 

Casino Royale never really tried as much. 


Overall the selection of filming location and studio set up for Skyfall was much better. The bright and vibrant colors for Macau & Shanghai. The drab colors of Silva’s island. Then you had the dark shots in Scotland with lots of highlights with the explosions.

When it comes to physical 4k media on home projectors and TVs… Skyfall is definitely a more visually stunning watch.
  - Skyfall had Roger Deakins as cinematographer, didn’t realize that until recently
    - Roger Deakins prefers to shoot digital
3. In all objective senses digital should be better. However, this technical superiority can lead to the idea of everything being recoverable in post and in a way scenes being shot a little lazily. With film, there is more pressure to get as much correct as possible in the original shot. The film-look  is really imperfections of highlights and dark shadows caused by it's more rigid nature. This can give film a more natural look.
  - 100% this. 

Digital cameras can capture the same look and feel as film, but what you said is the real factor often missed. 

The finiteness of film means if you missed it… you missed it. That “liveness” is felt on set. From a focus puller to the actors. Nothing taken for granted. 

That’s the spirit I’ve experienced when I’ve shot on film vs digital. I love digital for all the conveniences, but shooting film is experiential. 

That connection between me and that singular moment is real.
4. Film is always better

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. A Latin American Impression of PTA’s One Battle After Another Prologue

**Score:** 67
**Body:** I recently watched PTA’s OBAA, and I know the subreddit is already full of opinions on it, so forgive me if this has already been discussed.
One of the things that stuck with me most is how weirdly… Latin American–core the whole prologue feels.
  
I’ve lost count of how many films from my homeland (Brazil) I’ve seen that follow the same structure—period pieces recounting the fortunes and misfortunes (mostly misfortunes, honestly) of armed revolutionary groups during the military dictatorship of the 1960s to 1980s. Down to the recurring dilemma of “we’re not killers, but when shit hits the fan, what do you do?”
  
I know the film is loosely based on Pynchon’s Vineland, but from what I’ve read, its background is more about the free-love hippie culture of the ’60s. PTA’s Perfidia Beverly Hills, however, is a fascinating character who invites a broad and still-controversial debate about women’s roles and experiences in Latin American paramilitary revolutionary organizations (the Uruguayan Tupamaros women revolutionaries immediately came to mind), particularly regarding the abdication of motherhood.
  
Again, I haven’t read Vineland, nor do I know the exact degree of its relatability with U.S. civil rights or revolutionary organizations of that era, but the prologue of One Battle After Another hits every single note for me from a Latin American perspective.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The French 75 seems to be loosely based on The Weather Underground, and the groups that followed in their wake. The WU started by bombing unoccupied federal buildings, then moved on to more violent activity. 

They inspired a series of (less famous) groups, who did bank robberies and were generally more violent. 

But these groups were similar, in my understanding to a lot of the South American revolutionary groups of the time—who inspired who is beyond my knowledge. 

As another commenter said, the L American experience of strong man leaders does map neatly onto Trump and a lot of our current politics. So the parallels you’re seeing make sense to me!
  - Incidentally, Lebowski was a member of ‘the Seattle 7’, a fake group based on the real ‘Seattle Liberation Front’, so he and Bob have more in common that wearing bathrobes and smoking weed lol
    - The Big Lebowski has like this whole allegorical layer about tons of characters being old and jaded versions of different movements of the 1960s (leftist revolutionary, Vietnam veteran, Free Love/sexual liberation, etc.). But none of them really accomplished much of anything and they don't really know what to do with that fact, so they're all just living in the past (but in different pasts) while lazing around.
      - Yes! I love the way the Coens tuck this stuff into their movies. The Dude and Walter are also always quoting George Bush Sr, the language he was using around promoting Gulf War 1. The relationship between the US and Iraq sorta mirrors the interactions between the Lebowskis, too, from a left perspective. 

And in No Country for Old Men, they do something similar--all the men are veterans of different wars, and that really informs their approach to the world, and also speaks to the 'transferrable skills' of military involvement really being directly translatable to a life of law enforcement \*or\* crime. It's not directly addressed in the film, much moreso in the novel. But I think it still made it into the film, if not on a surface level.
        - I always think of the quote from Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas about the failure of the 60s to really accomplish much.

>And that, I think, was the handle—that sense of inevitable victory over the forces of Old and Evil. ...there was no point in fighting—on our side or theirs. We had all the momentum; we were riding the crest of a high and beautiful wave. . . .So now, less than five years later, you can go up on a steep hill in Las Vegas and look West, and with the right kind of eyes you can almost see the high-water mark—that place where the wave finally broke and rolled back.
  - Hey, that’s nice to know, already liked the fellas!  
  
Brazilian revolutionary forces actualy split into urban and rural guerillas. I’d say the urban guerillas had more in common with the WU (bank ‘expropriations’ and so on), while rural ones were often more aligned with maoist and cuban models.  
  
They were all massacred and some lucky ones were able to go into exile, by the way.
    - The WU adopted tactics from Marighella’s ‘Minimanual of the Urban Guerilla’ so there’s a lot of Latin American influence to be found in America at the time.

I’d love to know if PTA went directly to the source or picked up his vibe through yankee revolutionaries, but the connection is there either way.
2. I’d say it’s certainly a pertinent parallel, especially as American politics is becoming increasingly similar to South America, with its Presidential branch absorbing more and more power and veering into authoritarianism in much the same way as many Central and South American countries have.

Trump’s political appeal has a lot in common with South American populism, where the domain of politics and political discourse ceases to be about policy and starts to become a matter of personality and representation; Trump representing a specific kind of machismo that appeals to many voters: he’s “their guy,” who they feel.
 can vouch for their interests. Whether or not they actually agree with his racist, nationalist, isolationist agenda is shockingly irrelevant.
  - That was precisely the sort of commentary I was getting from the film, 100%.
3. Really random note but did anyone else notice how a lot of the cars/vehicles aren't contemporary? Seems like everyone is driving stuff from the 90s or something. Not sure if it means but it seems very intentional.

## 2. Why is Pulp Fiction considered “Meta” or “Self Aware”?

**Score:** 42
**Body:** I keep hearing this notion that Pulp Fiction was famous because it was self-aware. Characters are doing things outside their roles and that apparently makes it a "meta-movie."

8 1/2 or Holy Mountain are older movies that are much more "this is a movie that we're making", right? More recently, something like Deadpool is very explicitly meta.

But Pulp Fiction just seems like a fun movie. The gangsters are having fun. That doesn’t make the movie self aware. So what's the big deal with Pulp Fiction as more than just a really fun film?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pulp Fiction is self aware, but the characters aren't. It's meta because the film doesn't mind if you notice that it's a film. The most obvious example of this is Mia Wallace drawing a square on the screen, but it's everywhere. It's in the way the camera impatiently waits outside the door while Jules and Vincent walk further down the corridor to talk about foot massages. It's in getting John Travolta to dance just like he used to. It's in not showing anyone what's inside the briefcase or why Marsellus has a plaster on the back of his neck, knowing that audiences will obsess over those details. The film wants the audience to remember that they're watching a film, where most films try to hide that fact to fully immerse the viewer.
  - The best comparison is to Godard's *Band of Outsiders* - who Tarantino named his production company after.
  - This is a really great way of putting it.
  - There’s also the rear screen projection in the cab car being black and white.
2. Pulp Fiction can be seen as metatextual, not so much ‘meta” in the sense of self-aware (the most likely exception being Mia’s “don’t be a 🔲”). So you’ve got John Travolta dancing, for example, and allusions and references to various noirs and New Wave movies etc - visual “quotes” or samples that Tarantino throws in as he sees fit. It doesn’t mean the characters know they’re in a movie, it means (if you like) that the movie ‘knows’ it’s a movie and that it exists as part of a history of movies. The viewer doesn’t need to know that this scene is from Bonnie & Clyde, or that one’s from The Warriors, or that Jules’ Bible speech is in fact a Sonny Chiba crib - but the movie knows.

Cabin in the Woods is a similar example - it doesn’t break the fourth wall, nobody knows they’re just a character on the screen, but the whole thing is founded on horror movie metatextuality - it plays with, subverts and comments on the tropes of the genre. Pulp fiction does that for, well, pulp cinema, by and large.
3. One of the interesting things about Pulp Fiction is how it has aged.

When you look back and ask what are the most influential films of all time, films that singlehandedly changed the course of the history of cinema, Pulp Fiction is right up there along with Star Wars, Bonnie & Clyde, Breathless, and The Matrix. When it came out it felt like a goddamned EXPLOSION - a dynamic, lurid, breath of fresh air, style, and vision that completely upended the independent film scene. There were so many PF knockoffs, witty snarky crime thriller/comedies filled with pop cultural references, dutch angles and experimental storytelling, and lots of outlandish comedic violence. It and "Clerks" ushered in this new era of independent cinema, film festivals, and more, all of which trickled up into mainstream Hollywood (Tarantino famously did an uncredited rewrite on Crimson Tide, for instance). There had never been a movie that was so in love with other movies, and not just "fancy" movies but the low rent 70's revenge exploitation films that Tarantino saw as a kid (and would later write about in his book "Cinema Speculation" which is a really interesting window into how audiences thought about those films contemporaneously). Tarantino was the first movie nerd director, paving the way for Robert Rodriguez, Guillermo Del Toro, and even helping elevate the Coen Brothers who reached all-new heights around the same time with their film "Fargo." It was all of a piece, all part of this explosion of interesting new American cinema, and Pulp Fiction was the flashpoint that ignited it all.

OK so how has it aged? Well after ~~20~~ 30 years of Pulp Fiction basically redefining what was allowed and expected in independent cinema, after ~~20~~ 30 years of Pulp Fiction's children trying to outdo each other with more and more snappy dialogue and outlandish violence and random film references (including subsequent Tarantino films!), when you watch it today it feels VERY subdued, subtle, and tame. It's weird how quiet and thoughtful and slow it is. Its reputation has always been this insanely energetic and muscular filmmaking by a stylish superstar, but it's this really gently filmed and subtly made movie.

All this to say I can see why you are asking your question. It is a very meta and self-aware film, but since it has been eclipsed by absolutely unreal levels of meta and self-awareness since, it doesn't feel that way anymore.
  - I agree with what you said & I hate to be the one to say it, but it's been 30 years now.
  - >Tarantino was the first movie nerd director, 


I disagree with this point. The New Hollywood movement were the first generation of filmmakers raised on films and wearing those influences on their sleeves. Star Wars and Indiana Jones are strong forebearers of what Tarantino would later do. 
    - I think there is a distinction between the Film School Generation of directors like Lucas, Spielberg, and Scorcese, and the Movie Nerd Director of the 90's. When I think of directors like Tarantino and Rodriguez and Kevin Smith, I also think about websites like Ain't It Cool News. A different flavor of nerddom.

I did not mean to say Tarantino was the first director to be a nerd about movies, but he was the first major success of the AICN generation of directors.

In fact I think there's a paper to be written (perhaps someone has written it) about the confluence of the 90's indie explosion and the rise of the World Wide Web and how it allowed anyone to write about and promote films they loved. I don't think you get one without the other.
4. It is a really fun film, but 'Pulp Fiction' is also appreciated for making a strong point on how properties intrinsic to the filmmaking art, such as editing, create meaning that transcends the traditional ideia of a fixed plot structure. The fact 'Pulp Fiction' does this while also being deliberately accessible by working with traditional B movie tropes (martial arts, blaxploitation and so on), while also not being shy on subverting said tropes, is something that makes it stand out from films like 'Memento', for instance, which takes itself more seriously at the risk of alienating part of the audience.

## 3. OBAA is PTA’s most autobiographical film to date

**Score:** 3
**Body:** I am not going to make the full comparison but I will set the stage in hopes those of you who have seen it will see it again with a new lens. If you have not seen this film do not read and go see it.

Some thoughts after sitting with the film for a few days.

OBAA is very precise in the periods which it is set in. The opening of the movie takes place 16 years prior to what we assume is modern day, which would be 2007-2009 (depending on if you are thinking about when the movie was written, filmed, or premiered). Ghetto Pat is an explosives expert who primary responsibility is to create a show while the rest of the resistance fighters do the real work for the revolution. To reiterate this, he is the great Showman.

Moving to PTA’s real life, at this time he was premiering a film named There Will Be Blood, a deeply anti-Bush era film focused on the relationship between Capitalism and Religion, and the men who lead these movements. TWBB was crowned and heralded as on of the most important films of its era. It goes without saying that this was the beginning of the Obama era, which we were hopeful would leave to a long progressive movement in the US.

__

In the next section of the movie we see Ghetto Pat be forced to change his name to Bob after the movement failed and members were being purged. In this time, Bob retreats into a silo and raises his daughter and focuses on raising her while becoming crippling addicted to drugs (weed and alcohol are shown on screen but we can assume there may have been more at play).  During this time, the US has fallen prey to a deeply Facistic rule which led to racial segregation, demonization of non-whites, and 
a militarized police force preying on citizens.

Since TWBB and The Master, PTA has completely retreated from filmmaking with a direct political edge to it. I will not make assumptions on his drug use, but there are stories out there for you to make your own conclusions. PTA got married and had children. His films were more focused on the internal workings of domestic relationships and people navigating them.

I’m not going to spend a paragraph explaining the political situation of the US, but we’re living in it yall.

__


I do not want this post to be a full explainer because I believe the message of this movie to be so moving that it needs to be felt and realized in the moment and not explained. But what I will say is PTA saw this moment as one where he feels the need to return to form in the hopes of saving a dying movement from the edge of something much more disturbing. His choice to end this dark story in such a hopeful way makes this film be in the conversation for most important of the 21st century, if not the most.

## 5. Will there ever be another 2001: A Space Odyssey?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** In the history of film, *2001* stands out as uniquely ahead of its time. The filmmaking prowess on display is almost alien when you compare it to other works from 1968, and it still holds up today. Hell, behind-the-scenes videos of the rotating hallway in *Inception* still go viral on social media and Kubrick was somehow doing it **four decades** prior.

Do you think there could still be a film as revolutionary as *2001* in the modern generation? I feel like with all of the advancements in CGI and VFX, the ceiling is almost too high. What would such a film even look like, that it would hold up for the next forty years? The only films that I can think of that really come close are *Interstellar* (not necessarily that it's the most technologically advanced film since then, but I feel like Nolan perfected the IMAX experience in such a way that hasn't really been topped since), *Into the Spiderverse* (for opening a new chapter of animated film and stopgapping the slew of corporate 3D slop) and *Everything Everywhere All at Once* (for pushing the limits of what a relatively-small-budget indie film can achieve). But seriously, if a film from this generation came out that would still be impressive 40 years later (not necessarily for VFX but for any filmmaking techniques) what would it look like?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. CGI and VFX are just a very small, almost inconsequential component for a film today to achieve what 2001 did. It was Kubrick's vision, the themes he explored, the tension he had the audience hold on to, visual story telling---it was all technique. Not to mention the camera work, composition, use of color and set pieces. The general assumption that film quality and advancing technology have a linear relationship is just....completely wrong. 

Everything Everywhere All at Once was a great film not for the technology it employed but the filmmaking choices it went with (e.g., non-linear narration, multi-verse, surrealism etc..)

To answer your question I would say I dont think it's possible to achieve what 2001 did *in the way* it did. However, I can imagine a scenario where we develop a new technology for more immersive story telling experiences (e.g., VR).
  - I mostly agree and I probably should've been a bit clearer in my post. *2001* is one of my favorite films for all the reasons you mentioned, I just wanted to focus on the technical aspect of things because I found it particularly interesting today. 

There's a huge number of films that are undoubtedly some of the greatest of all time that aren't technically impressive and there are also countless films that are technological marvels and absolutely terrible. However, in the lineage of films that attempt and accomplish both, I think *2001* still stands out and incredibly ahead-of-its-time on both fronts and I wonder which films in the present day we in the future will look back on similarly.
    - I think Denis Villeneuve may have something up his sleeve once he wraps up Dune....Speaking of, from a sound engineering and practical/vfx perspective is way more noteworthy than Interstellar.

But....you are essentially asking "What juice is their left to squeeze?" I think 2001 stands out because so much of what Kubrick accomplished was from the early early era of filmmaking. It's the same reason that perhaps Citizen Kane still gets respect.....Over the years we have *squeezed the juice* without much **leaps** of development despite gradual technological **shifts**. He just got the absolute most from what was available......

Now, I am thinking of something like Toy Story where if you rewatch it---go ahead, check out 1 and 4.... That franchise has stood exceptionally well and has spawned a new era in digital animation in the early 90s and TS 1 still looks pretty good! I think we may look back with more respect for what it accomplished
2. I think that to focus on the technological aspect  - even in an effects-heavy picture like this - is to miss the point. If there is merit to 2001: A Space Odyysey - and I must confess such merit is largely lost on me - surely it's to be found in something more intangiable than the panache with which the effects were pulled off?

And while I concede that it looks highly accoplished - the Todd-AO format combined with Kubrick's insistence to avoid generational loss where possible and his general meticulousness - it would be wrong to say that almost 60 years later, every shot is beyond all possible reproach. The slit-scan sequences are inventive - though I think they run much too long - but nobody in good conscience would call them photorealistic, and Kubrick's attempt to grade aerial shots of Scotland and Monument valley into alien landscapes obviously only goes so far. There's some obviously front-projection in the Dawn of Man sequence, and the orbiting space stations don't rotate but merely glide across the frame in a manner that really shows that they are still photographs. None of this is a major problem with the film, again primarily because if it is succesfull it's for some deeper reason.

And no, I would say another film like it cannot be made again. It's very much a 1960 Cinerama movie. We don't have Cinerama anymore, and that doesn't just mean the format but certain conventions that went with that kind of presentation.
3. I feel as though with VFX we’ve now reached the point where we can put anything on screen that we can imagine, and have it look good. Therefore the only limitations are in the film-makers’ imaginations. 

Of course that depends on your definition of “looks good”, and also on whether you think it’s a good thing that anything is now possible. Plenty of iconic film moments were the results of work-arounds, because their first choice of what to put on screen wasn’t possible. Would Jaws have been better if they’d been able to get that damned model shark to work properly? I doubt it. Even 2001 has work-arounds. Kubrick thought the rocket effects looked crap, so the spaceships in 2001 just glide.

While I’m frequently amazed by modern films, it’s been a while since I’ve been amazed at the SFX/VFX. Unlike in 1968, we’re so used to everything looking great these days. I can't imagine what a big budget "new 2001" would have to do to stand out. That's just me though. Hopefully others can, and some of those people are film-makers.
4. In the age of CGI you'll find it hard to wow audiences based on visuals alone. What used to take problem solving, imagination, creativity and "movie magic" can be achieved by throwing money at effects houses employing thousands. Nobody is leaving the theatre asking each other "how did they do that?!" anymore like with 2001 because the answer is simple.

  
What would it take to get back to that? Storytelling will have to do the heavy lifting, possibly something surreal and odd enough as well, though that might alienate most viewers. Going outside the screen itself is another option, making films specifically for something like 4DX could be the new IMAX/3D experience.
5. What do you mean by "another"?

There's always going to be great films and what makes a film great, in large part, is the uniqueness of that film. So to ask for "another 2001" is kind of defeating what made 2001 great in the first place. 2001 is simultaneously a landmark magnum opus for Kubrick, and a time-capsule of what people valued in the time in which it was made. It needs no "follow-up".

Even so, there was a "follow-up"-- 2010: The Year We Make Contact (1985?). It was an OK film and worth watching, but no where near the level of 2001 in terms of art-- it was just half-decent multiplex fodder. 

It sounds like "VFX" (visual special effects?) are important to you. If that's the case, I would recommend a recent Chinese film: "The Wandering Earth II". This film has serious shortcomings (paper-thin character, unintentionally ludicrous dialog) but it's an incredible hyper-kinetic explosion of VFX unlike anything I've ever seen. It has the superficial characteristics of a Chinese film with anodyne heroes and bland characters (probably "required" for mass-market release in China), but there's deeper secondary themes in there that were fascinating.
  - Strangely I kind of love 2010 because it’s Arther C Clarkes follow up to explain what everything in 2001 - but it loses all the majesty and poetry of 2001 worth watching if you want to explore the 2001 universe some more but very dated now too
6. Lots of films have cool effects, Kubrick getting Arthur C. Clarke involved can not be understated. A key part of the film is the vision from Clarke, Kubrick with Clarke managed to pull of one of if not the best hard science fiction films made.

A lot of films have cool effects, they dont have the vision or experience to truly pull hard SF off. Interstellar is empty, it has the effects but not the ideas. If anything it's just a copy of 2001 A Space Odyssey, a re branded remake.

Arthur C. Clarke is one of the people who defined hard science fiction, he's the gold standard I judge all hard SF books by.

Id love to know more about how they worked out the story, working out the practicality of space travel & how to depict it in a film. The talks on how rotation is needed for Centrifugal force to reproduce gravity, then to work out how to best communicate that in film and how to depict it in effects and sets. Teams of people working on how to create a vision, the quality control and perfection of the craftsmanship.

It's all the small things that most films gloss over, the touches that a casual viewer may miss but a SF fan get's so excited seeing they cant help but rant about.

I relay wish The Expanse had leaned more in to hard SF, it's close but always leans to pulp SF over hard.

PS shout out to 2010: The Year We Make Contact, tends to be forgotten but fairly good & not far from the books for a film.
7. I mean they do reboots after 20-30 years pretty much all the time (I guess Devil Wears Prada would be the latest victim?) so yeah I think we should get 2002: A Space Reckoning relatively soon.
